text: New medRxiv preprint from the URISN team: neighborhood deprivation index and seasonal respiratory infection rates across 55 Baltimore census tracts. Three respiratory seasons, de-identified data. Link in thread.

Surveillance data note: the neighborhood-level incidence gradient in our URISN sample is steeper than national estimates would predict. Suggests either undercount in national data or genuine local clustering. Working through the model implications.
New URISN preprint from @GeraldRomanPhD and team. Neighborhood deprivation and respiratory infection — important work for Baltimore and beyond.
At the Mid-Atlantic Epi Symposium today presenting URISN Year 2 spatial findings. Questions about sentinel-site selection strategy afterward were the best part of the Q&A.
Great talk by @GeraldRomanPhD at the Mid-Atlantic Epi Symposium. The spatial heterogeneity findings have real policy implications for neighborhood-level intervention targeting.
URISN Year 1 paper accepted at the Journal of Urban Health Equity. First-author. Months of revision, finally through. Thank you to Dr. Voss and the whole CIPH team.
Congratulations to @GeraldRomanPhD on the JUHE acceptance. Meridian-funded research making an impact.

On the relationship between environmental monitoring density and surveillance study power: we have three times the ambient air quality sensors in wealthier Baltimore neighborhoods. Worth considering when interpreting neighborhood-level health estimates.
@GeraldRomanPhD The sensor density finding is consistent with our modeling work. Differential measurement error across strata complicates the hierarchical model — happy to discuss.

URISN Year 2 interim report is public. Sentinel sites expanded to 19; preliminary spatial modeling suggests infection clustering is not explained by deprivation alone — built environment covariates warranted. Link: ciph.org/publications
Important work on urban respiratory illness surveillance from @GeraldRomanPhD and @CIPH_Baltimore. The URISN methodology is a model for neighborhood-scale infectious disease monitoring.

Presenting at the Urban Health Equity Forum in DC on March 19. Will discuss URISN findings and implications for neighborhood-level public health planning. Open to public health practitioners — registration at the UHEF site.

Co-authored paper published in Epidemiology and Community Health: Bayesian hierarchical small-area estimation of seasonal respiratory infection rates. Lead methodologist @MarcusHayesStat. ciph.org/publications
New in Journal of Urban Health Equity: socioeconomic patterning of respiratory infection burden in Baltimore — @GeraldRomanPhD et al. URISN study. Open access.
Abstract accepted for NCEpi Chicago, Oct 15-17. I will present the URISN Year 2 spatial analysis. First national conference presentation for this work — looking forward to the conversation.
Reminder that de-identified aggregate surveillance data can still reveal sensitive neighborhood patterns. Worth thinking carefully about table-1 granularity even in public-facing reports.
URISN Year 3 renewal submitted to Meridian PHF. Proposed expansion to 31 sentinel sites and integration of satellite-derived air quality covariates. Fingers crossed.
Also: finished the Bach fingerstyle arrangements workshop at Dragonfly this weekend. Completely different parts of my brain. Both exhausting and good.

On data granularity and interpretability in public health surveillance: the same table that helps a practitioner target outreach can expose a neighborhood to stigma. Not a reason to suppress the data, but a reason to publish carefully.

Working through the environmental covariate integration question for URISN Year 3. The NASA MERRA-2 PM2.5 series aligns decently with sentinel-site windows; NO2 is messier. Anyone used satellite-derived air quality in a mixed-effects model at this scale?
@GeraldRomanPhD We used MERRA-2 for a similar spatial model — temporal aggregation window matters a lot. Happy to share the preprocessing pipeline if useful.
